topics.title: Spring Data JPA & Hibernate — Entity Lifecycle, N+1, Caching
topics.description: Hibernate is the JPA provider that maps Java objects to database tables. Spring Data JPA wraps Hibernate with repository abstractions (JpaRepository, @Query). Understanding entity lifecycle (transient → managed → detached → removed), lazy vs eager loading, and the N+1 problem is critical for writing performant data-access code. First-level cache (Session) prevents duplicate queries within a transaction; second-level cache (Ehcache/Redis) caches across transactions.
topics.story: Hibernate is like a smart personal assistant who remembers everything you asked about today (1st level cache / persistence context). If you ask for the same customer twice in one conversation, she doesn't call the database again — she remembers. But each conversation is fresh (new transaction). The N+1 problem is like asking 'give me all orders' (1 query) and then for each order asking 'who is the customer?' (N queries) — 101 DB calls for 100 orders instead of 1 JOIN. @EntityGraph or JOIN FETCH is like asking your assistant 'bring me all orders WITH their customers' in one trip.
topics.analogy: EntityManager = personal assistant. Persistence Context = assistant's notebook for this session. MANAGED state = object in the notebook (auto-saves changes). DETACHED state = object removed from notebook (changes not tracked). @Transactional = the duration of one conversation. LazyLoading = 'I'll get the details when you ask'. EagerLoading = 'I'll bring everything upfront'. N+1 = 100 separate trips to the filing cabinet instead of one.
topics.memoryAnchor: TMDL lifecycle — Transient (new object, JPA does not know it exists) → Managed (EntityManager tracks it, dirty-check auto-saves at commit) → Detached (session/transaction closed, changes are ignored) → Removed (scheduled for DELETE at commit). N+1 TRAP: 1 query for a list of 100 orders + 100 separate queries for each order's items = 101 queries. Fix toolkit: JOIN FETCH in JPQL, @EntityGraph on repository method, @BatchSize on the relation, or DTO projections with constructor expressions. LAZY vs EAGER hook: LAZY = proxy placeholder, loaded only when you access the field. EAGER = loads immediately, always. Default: @OneToMany is LAZY. @ManyToOne is EAGER. L1 cache = Session (per transaction, always on). L2 cache = SessionFactory (optional, shared, needs configuration).
topics.firstPrinciples: ORM maps Object model ↔ Relational model. The impedance mismatch: objects have identity (==), state (fields), behavior (methods), and inheritance. Tables have rows, columns, and foreign keys. Hibernate bridges this with the persistence context: a unit-of-work pattern that tracks entity changes and flushes them as SQL at transaction commit.
topics.bruteForce: Without ORM: JDBC — write SQL strings, manage ResultSet, manually map columns to fields. Every entity needs its own mapper. Update = re-fetch + modify + manually write UPDATE SQL. Association loading = manual JOIN query and manual result assembly. Works but 10x more code for complex models.
topics.optimizedApproach: @Entity User + @OneToMany List<Order> orders. Spring Data: UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User,Long>. findByEmail(String) auto-generates query. findAll(Pageable) for pagination. @Query('SELECT u FROM User u JOIN FETCH u.orders WHERE u.id = :id') for N+1 fix. @Transactional on service methods for atomic operations.
topics.whenToUse: LAZY (default for @OneToMany/@ManyToMany): use when associations not always needed. EAGER (@ManyToOne default): small, always-needed associations. Fix N+1: JOIN FETCH when you know you'll always need the association; @EntityGraph for optional eager loading per query. @Transactional at service layer (not repository — too granular, not controller — too broad). For bulk inserts: saveAll() with batch_size config. For reporting queries: native SQL or JDBC Template.

examples.title: Entity Lifecycle, Dirty Checking & Lazy Loading
examples.explanation: Dirty checking is Hibernate's magic: managed entities are snapshotted at load; at transaction commit Hibernate compares current state to snapshot and generates UPDATE SQL for changed fields. You never call update() — just modify the managed object. LAZY loading defers DB access until the collection is accessed — good for performance but requires an open session (LazyInitializationException outside transaction).
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: Topic entity loaded → modify title field → @Transactional method ends → Hibernate auto-generates UPDATE. User + submissions: @OneToMany LAZY → only load submissions when explicitly requested. @Transactional on service methods ensures session stays open for LAZY loading. readOnly=true skips dirty check snapshot for read-only operations.

examples.title: N+1 Problem — Detection & Fix with JOIN FETCH
examples.explanation: N+1 is the most common JPA performance killer: fetching N parents each triggering 1 child query = N+1 total. JOIN FETCH loads the entire graph in one SQL JOIN. @EntityGraph is cleaner for ad-hoc eager loading. DISTINCT prevents duplicate parents when join produces multiple rows. Pagination with Pageable + @Query is essential for large datasets.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner Topics with Examples: findAllWithExamples() avoids N+1 when rendering topic list with preview. ProblemsPage: Pageable for pagination. Submissions with User: JOIN FETCH when generating performance analytics. Always check Hibernate SQL logs (show-sql: true in dev) to detect N+1.

problems.title: Custom Repository Query with Pagination
problems.hint1: Method name follows Spring Data convention: findBy + FieldName + OrderBy + FieldName + Asc.
problems.hint2: Add Pageable as the last parameter to get a Page<T> result.
problems.testCases: Returns Page<Product> filtered by category, sorted by price ascending
problems.sampleInput: findAll() on 100 orders each with 5 items (lazy loading)
problems.sampleOutput: Without fix: 101 queries. With JOIN FETCH: 1 query.

problems.title: Detect and Fix N+1
problems.hint1: findAll() = 1 query for departments. d.employees.size() for each dept = N queries.
problems.hint2: Fix: add @Query('SELECT DISTINCT d FROM Department d LEFT JOIN FETCH d.employees') to repository.
problems.hint3: DISTINCT prevents duplicate Department rows from the join.
problems.testCases: findAllWithEmployees() generates 1 SQL query instead of 1+N
problems.sampleOutput: SELECT name, email FROM users WHERE department_id=5 (only 2 columns, not SELECT *)

problems.title: Optimistic Locking with @Version
problems.hint1: Add @Version Long version; field to the entity.
problems.hint2: JPA adds WHERE version=? to every UPDATE statement. If version changed, 0 rows updated → OptimisticLockException.
problems.hint3: Simulate: load entity twice (two separate EntityManager sessions), update first, then try to update second — second should fail.
problems.sampleInput: Two threads concurrently decrement stock from 10 (version=1)
problems.sampleOutput: First update: version=2 OK. Second: OptimisticLockException (stale version=1)
